coredata.dc:title: Design of graphene quantum dots decorated MnO2 nanosheet based fluorescence turn “On-Off-On” nanoprobe for highly sensitive detection of lactoferrin
coredata.dc:creator: Nangare, Sopan
Patil, Sagar
Patil, Sairendhri
Khan, Zamir
Patil, Ashwini
Patil, Pravin
coredata.dc:description: Lactoferrin estimation is increasingly acquiring prominence as a novel biomarker for the diagnosis of periodontal disease. To date, diverse lactoferrin detection methods which include electrochemical, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, colorimetric, and others have been extensively portrayed. Unfortunately, these systems have significant shortcomings including low sensitivity, selectivity, high cost, arduous and time-consuming technique, and so forth. Recently, the fluorescence-based method shows remarkable uniqueness that overcomes the demerits of traditionally reported techniques. Therefore, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2-NS) based simplistic, highly sensitive, and selective fluorescent turn ‘Off-On’ mediated GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe was designed. Herein, MnO2-NS addition demonstrated the quenching of GQDs containing fluorescence through inner filter effects (IFE) and strong interaction between GQDs and MnO2-NS. The lactoferrin addition destroyed the MnO2-NS and fluorescence emission of GQDs reappeared which may be because of redox reaction between lactoferrin and prepared MnO2-NS. Herein, nanoprobe offers a wide concentration range and low limit of detection of 5 to 1600 ng/mL and 1.69 ng/mL, respectively. As fabricated GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe sensor demonstrated high selectivity, good stability, and reproducibility towards lactoferrin that assuring applicability of biosensor. Therefore, the GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe will offer a simplistic sensor with adequate sensitivity to achieve highly responsive and selective detection of lactoferrin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lactoferrin
Periodontal disease
Graphene quantum dots
Manganese dioxide nanosheet
Fluorescent sensor
Sensitivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Green synthesis of graphene quantum dots
Fabrication of GQDs and MnO2-Ns conjugate (GQDs@MnO2-NS)
Spectroscopical characterizations of GQDs, MnO2-NS, and GQDs@MnO2-NS
Selectivity study and other analytical features
Sensing of lactoferrin using GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Hydrothermal green synthesis of GQDs from wood apple shells. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Images of synthesized GQDs observed in the UV cabinet at a different light wavelength (A), UV–vis spectra of synthesized GQDs, MnO2-NS and GQDs@MnO2-NS (B), Fluorescence excitation spectra, and emission spectrum of processed GQDs (C),(D) GQDs emission spectra with excitation wavelengths ranging from 310 nm to 360 nm.

Particle size of green synthesized GQDs (A), MnO2-Ns (B), and GQDs@MnO2- Ns (C). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Zeta potential analysis of GQDs (A), MnO2-Ns (B), and GQDs@MnO2- Ns (C).

FTIR spectra of green synthesized GQDs (A), MnO2-NS (B), and GQDs@MnO2-NS (C). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
HRTEM images of synthesized GQDs (A), MnO2-NS (B), and GQDs@MnO2-NS (C).
Quenching of fluorescence of GQDs using different concentrations of prepared MnO2-NS (1 µg/mL to 110 µg/mL) (A), Graph between MnO2-NS concentration added vs quenching efficiency (B).

Emission spectra of synthesized GQDs as a function of lactoferrin concentration from 5 ng/mL to 1600 ng/mL (A), Calibration curve based on the concentration of lactoferrin and regained quenched fluorescence of GQDs (B).
Presentation of selectivity response of prepared GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe in presence of lactoferrin and other interfering agents.
Sensing of lactoferrin using GQDs@MnO2-NS-based fluorescent nanoprobe.
Real-time analysis of lactoferrin in artificial saliva.

head.ce:title: Design of graphene quantum dots decorated MnO2 nanosheet based fluorescence turn “On-Off-On” nanoprobe for highly sensitive detection of lactoferrin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur 425405, Dist: Dhule (MS), India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur 425405, Dist: Dhule (MS), India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur 425405, Dist: Dhule (MS), India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur 425405, Dist: Dhule (MS), India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology, R. C. Patel Institute of Arts, Commerce and Science College, Shirpur 425405, Dist: Dhule (MS), India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: R. C. Patel Institute of Arts, Commerce and Science College

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microbiology, R. C. Patel Institute of Arts, Commerce and Science College, Shirpur 425405, Dist: Dhule (MS), India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lactoferrin estimation is increasingly acquiring prominence as a novel biomarker for the diagnosis of periodontal disease. To date, diverse lactoferrin detection methods which include electrochemical, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, colorimetric, and others have been extensively portrayed. Unfortunately, these systems have significant shortcomings including low sensitivity, selectivity, high cost, arduous and time-consuming technique, and so forth. Recently, the fluorescence-based method shows remarkable uniqueness that overcomes the demerits of traditionally reported techniques. Therefore, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2-NS) based simplistic, highly sensitive, and selective fluorescent turn ‘Off-On’ mediated GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe was designed. Herein, MnO2-NS addition demonstrated the quenching of GQDs containing fluorescence through inner filter effects (IFE) and strong interaction between GQDs and MnO2-NS. The lactoferrin addition destroyed the MnO2-NS and fluorescence emission of GQDs reappeared which may be because of redox reaction between lactoferrin and prepared MnO2-NS. Herein, nanoprobe offers a wide concentration range and low limit of detection of 5 to 1600 ng/mL and 1.69 ng/mL, respectively. As fabricated GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe sensor demonstrated high selectivity, good stability, and reproducibility towards lactoferrin that assuring applicability of biosensor. Therefore, the GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe will offer a simplistic sensor with adequate sensitivity to achieve highly responsive and selective detection of lactoferrin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Periodontal disease is common in many countries [1], and is frequently produced by microbial infection. It stimulates the adherence of connective tissue and the prevention of bone surrounding the teeth at the onset of illness [2,3]. Despite this, its following inflammatory response adds to the loss of periodontal tissues in a patient. As a result, it is a prolonged inflammatory illness in people that causes not only regional mouth diseases but also systemic organ abnormalities [3]. Importantly, periodontal disease if remain untreated, the illness progresses to gradual bone damage, resulting in tooth movement and eventual tooth loss. As per literature, periodontal disease affects more than half of the grownup people in the United States, with around 10% suffering from serious disease those results in earlier tooth loss [4]. To prevent additional severances of periodontal disease, it is critical to accurately diagnose it. In this regard, biomarker detection is essential in the prediction of health difficulties, and scientists are presently investigating novel biomarkers for sickness diagnosis. In latest days, advances in the science of diagnosing oral as well as periodontal illness have evolved into ways for measuring periodontal threats employing quantifiable evidence kind of as biomarkers [5].

Lactoferrin (family: transferrin) is an iron-binding glycoprotein found in secondary neutrophil granulocytes [6]. As per literature, it demonstrates responsiveness to acute inflammation [3]. In addition, lactoferrin is observed in tears and saliva [6]. Lactoferrin estimation has received a lot of attention during the last two decades as a new biomarker [7] for the diagnosis of periodontal disease. Furthermore, it may be recommended for the diagnosis of various inflammatory illnesses [8]. Several identification studies have proposed various approaches for lactoferrin detection. Mainly, single radial immunodiffusion [9], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [7], latex aggregation methods [10] Microfluidic paper-based analytical devices [11], etc. have been documented for the detection of lactoferrin. Herein, the reported immunoassay demonstrates a high sensitivity toward the lactoferrin which may be because of the preference of affinity receptors that demonstrates high affinity towards the lactoferrin [11]. Despite this, such methods are suffering from plentiful disadvantages including dedicated laboratory requirements, not being feasible for point of care analysis, needing high expertise, being expensive, and requiring several steps. In addition, most of these methods are suffering from a limitation of assay sophistication and depend primarily on workstation laboratory tools [7,11]. As a result, there is a tremendous demand to establish a competent substitute for detecting lactoferrin, which can contribute in the diagnosis of periodontal disease.

The detection of several sorts of analytes using various types of sensors is attracting a lot of attention from the scientific community [12–14]. For the construction of sensors, several types of nanomaterials have been revealed including graphene [15], metal-organic frameworks [16,17], borophene [18], black phosphorus [19], etc. Considering this, graphene-based materials are often favored because they provide analyte detection with an enhanced presentation [20]. Recently, the preference for fluorescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a nanoprobe for sensing biomarkers [21,22] may be because of their supreme and versatile physicochemical properties [23,24]. Owing to interesting optical properties as well as outstanding electrical conductivity, GQDs are extensively reported for optical applications as well as electrochemical applications [23,25]. To conquer the demerits of chemically synthesized GQDs, presently different natural precursors have been extensively preferred for the synthesis of GQDs [25]. A literature survey reported that the GQDs can demonstrate the different colors of fluorescence associated with reaction parameters and synthesis methods. The fluorescence of GQDs is serving a pivotal function in the detection of biomarkers in biosensing applications, with the fluorescence “turn Off” and “turn On” sensing processes being described [26]. For example, nitrogen-doped GQDs have been reported for sensing applications that demonstrate the ability to sense uric acid [27]. Furthermore, a functionalized GQDs-based biosensor with amazing sensitivity and selective monitoring of carcinoembryonic antigen has been disclosed [28]. Regardless, there is a precondition for improving the sensitivity and selectivity of GQD-based biosensors. Furthermore, using biomolecules with GQDs is pricey.

Scholars are now engaged in using inorganic materials such as manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2-NS) for monitoring purposes [29]. Importantly, MnO2-NS is a novel type of 2D nanomaterial with several advantages such as strong biocompatibility, high specific surface area, eco-friendliness, and so on [30]. In addition, it offers excellent light adsorption potential and provides a broad absorption peak. Fascinatingly, it furnishes the large spectral overlap due to an almost similar absorption spectrum. Literature reported the spectral overlapping of MnO2-NS with different carbon-based fluorescent materials [31,32]. Principally, MnO2-NS can oxidize the analyte whereas it gets reduced into biocompatible Mn2+ ions. Owing to this, it provides the fluorescence turn On ability to nanoprobe [33]. Zhou and co-authors reported the detection of glucose and hydrogen peroxide using a combination of graphitic-C3N4 nanosheet and MnO2 nanocomposite. Adsorption of Mn2+ on the surface of graphitic-C3N4 nanosheet has been possible due to the existence of amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of the nanosheet. Herein, the fluorescence of graphitic-C3N4 nanosheet has been quenched after the addition of MnO2 nanocomposite that because of fluorescence resonance energy transfer between nanosheet and nanocomposite [34]. In addition, a combination of fluorescent materials with MnO2-NS nanoprobes has been reported for the detection of ascorbic acid [32], acid phosphatase [33], glutathione [35], cholesterol [29], captopril [36], etc. that provides the speedy, simplistic, highly sensitive, and selective detection aptitude.

In this work, we developed the fluorescent GQDs@MnO2-NS-based nanoprobe for the highly sensitive detection of lactoferrin. Herein, GQDs were synthesized using wood apple as a precursor via the hydrothermal method. Then, GQDs@MnO2-NS conjugates were developed using a nanosheet of MnO2 through the sonochemical method. Finally, the applicability of GQDs@MnO2-NS conjugates as a fluorescent probe for the detection of lactoferrin was evaluated. This prepared GQDs@MnO2-NS conjugates mediated fluorescent nanoprobe provided the fluorescent turn “Off-On” sensing mechanism whereas high sensitivity and selectivity in presence of lactoferrin with acceptable results. As a result of achieving the stated goal of the study, we will be able to create unique, uncomplicated, efficient, label-free, non-invasive, extremely sensitive, and selective fluorescence nanoprobe for lactoferrin measurement.

The wood apple (Limonia Acidissima) was gathered from Shirpur, Dhule district (Maharashtra), India. Lactoferrin was provided as a gift sample from Friesl and Campina, Netherlands. Manganese chloride tetrahydrate (MnCl2·4H2O) was procured from Thermo fisher scientific Pvt. Ltd. Pune, India. Tetramethylammonium Hydroxide (TMA.OH) from Avra synthesis Pvt. Ltd. Telangana, India. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Private Limited, India. Double distilled water (DDW) was purchased from Avtar Performance Materials India Limited, Thane, Maharashtra. All analytical grade solvents were utilized as they arrived.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The formerly disclosed approach was used to accomplish the green synthesis of GQDs [37]. In brief, shells of wood apples were removed and cleaned with DDW to remove dust and particles. The shells were then processed in a research laboratory vacuum oven at 80 °C for 24 h before being ground into powder. This prepared wood apple shell powder was used to synthesize GQDs through the single-step hydrothermal method. Initially, wood apple powder (2 g) was stirred with 35 mL of DDW along with 15 mL of ethyl alcohol. The solution was then placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave in a stainless-steel hydrothermal reactor and then treated for 7 h in an oven at 160 °C. The autoclave was gently lowered to room temperature when the reaction ended. The yellow-white fluid changed to a dark brown solution throughout this hydrothermal phase, indicating the formation of GQDs from shell powder. At last, obtained GQDs were filtered to eliminate bigger size particles of ash residue using 2 µm filter paper followed by purification using a dialysis membrane (Fig. 1 ). After completion of dialysis, the obtained GQDs solution was processed in a hot air oven at 80 °C to yield a fine powder of pure synthesized GQDs s for further characterizations and design of fluorescent nanoprobe for lactoferrin sensing applications. The previously stated approach was used to compute the percent quantum yield [27]. The excitation and emission of GQDs were measured using a spectrofluorometer (JASCO FP8200 spectrofluorometer) whereas stable emission wavelength was checked using different excitation wavelengths from 310 nm to 350 nm.

In this segment, MnO2-NS were produced as formerly discussed [32]. In brief, a mixture of H2O2 (4 mL) and1 M TMA.OH (24 mL) was prepared by simple mixing into volumetric flack. After that, this prepared mixture was added into an aqueous solution of 0.3 M MnCl2·4H2O (20 mL) mixture with continuous stirring at 100 rpm at room temperature for 8 h. The resultant product was centrifuged at 12000 rpm using a cold centrifuge for 20 min at 25 °C to separate the MnO2-NS from the solution. The residue was then washed three times with a methanol/distilled water combination. The drying of residue was performed using a laboratory hot air oven at 60 °C to remove the solvent content. After complete drying, 50 mg residue was added to 100 mL water and sonicated for 2 h to generate separate MnO2-NS. The final product was obtained by separation of water from MnO2-NS using cold centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 30 min. The drying of the sample was achieved using a laboratory hot air oven at 60 °C. The resulting compound was employed for subsequent characterizations and conjugate construction using green-made GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQDs and MnO2-Ns conjugate (GQDs@MnO2-NS)
ce:section.ce:para: In this step, sonochemistry was used to fabricate the GQDs@MnO2-NS using synthesized GQDs and MnO2-NS [35]. In brief, 4 mg of dried GQDs (200 µg/ mL) were evenly dispersed in cleaned 50 volumetric flasks containing 20 mL of DDW. Then, 2 mg of as-prepared MnO2-NS powder was added to 50 mL of DDW using simple mixing. After that, different concentrations of MnO2-NS such as 1 µg/mL, to 110 µg/mL were added to confirm the complete quenching of fluorescence of GQDs. Finally, an optimized concentration (100 µg/mL) of MnO2-NS was added to the prepared GQDs solution and then subjected to ultrasonication for 10 min to achieve the nanoprobe. Finally, the quenching of GQDs fluorescence was obtained using a spectrofluorometer. GQDs@MnO2-NS conjugates were processed in a research laboratory oven at 50 °C to eliminate the solvent content before being employed for further characterization and measurement of lactoferrin as a fluorescent nanoprobe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectroscopical characterizations of GQDs, MnO2-NS, and GQDs@MnO2-NS
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized GQDs, MnO2-NS, and GQDs@MnO2-NS were characterized for different spectroscopical characterizations including UV–visible (vis) spectrophotometer, and Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Particle size analysis, UV chamber, spectrofluorometer, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). In brief, the UV–visible spectrum of the synthesized GQDs, MnO2-NS and GQDs@MnO2-NS were measured within 800–400 nm employing a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV 1800 Shimadzu, Japan) utilizing a quartz cuvette. The resultant GQDs, MnO2-NS, and GQDs@MnO2-NS were subjected to FT-IR spectra measurement using an FT-IR spectrophotometer (Agilent CARY 630 FT-IR). The fluorescence studies of GQDs, MnO2-NS, and GQDs@MnO2-NS were carried out in a UV compartment at various wavelengths (254 nm and 365 nm, as well as visible light). The particle size analysis, zeta potential determination, and polydispersity index (PDI) of synthesized GQDs, MnO2-NS, and GQDs@MnO2-NS were reported via Nanoplus 3 Particulate System (Micromeritics, USA). The shape, size, and crystalline nature of GQDs, MnO2-NS, and GQDs@MnO2-NS were assessed using HR-TEM (HR-TEM, Jeol/JEM 2100) with a LaB6 light source at 200 kV. For detecting lactoferrin and measuring other analytical parameters, a spectrofluorometer (JASCO FP8200 spectrofluorometer) was employed.

Accurately, 50 µg/mL of freshly prepared solution of GQDs@MnO2-NS fluorescent nanoprobe was prepared for sensitivity measurement. In brief, different concentrations of lactoferrin from 5 ng/mL to 1600 ng/mL were prepared using pH 6.8 phosphate buffer (10 mM), separately. After that, each prepared sample was added to the GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe and kept aside for 10 min to complete redox reaction. After achievement of a redox reaction, regain of fluorescent intensity of GQDs was measured using a spectrofluorometer. At last, the recovery of fluorescence intensity of GQDs was confirmed with respective concentrations confirmed by the stern-Volmer plot. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of lactoferrin were estimated using slope (m) and standard deviations (ϭ) [LOD: 3.3*ϭ/m, LOQ: 10* ϭ/m].

The real-time analysis of lactoferrin was performed using artificial saliva. In this step, artificial saliva was prepared as per the previously reported composition [38]. Following that, 10 ng/mL and 100 ng/mL lactoferrin were spiking into the 50 µg/mL of GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe and kept aside to complete the interaction. Finally, both samples were analyzed using the abovementioned method. In this, the percent recovery and percent relative standard deviation (% RSD) of lactoferrin were determined to ensure the suggested GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe's real-time applicability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity study and other analytical features
ce:section.ce:para: For selectivity study, different interfering agers were preferred. In brief, solutions of selected interfering agents such as K+, Ca2+, Na+, Zn2+, urea, uric acid, glucose, ascorbic acid, cysteine, lactoferrin, etc. were prepared using 100 ng/mL concentration in pH 6.8 phosphate buffer (10 mM), separately. Then, 100 µL of prepared solutions of each interfering agent were mixed with 2 mL of a solution of GQDs@MnO2-NS conjugates (50 µg/mL). The emission spectra of each sample were measured using a spectrofluorometer. For comparison, the 100 nM of concentration of lactoferrin was added to 2 mL of a solution of GQDs@MnO2-NS conjugates and measured for regained fluorescence intensity of GQDs. In addition, the mixture of all interfering agents with lactoferrin was checked for changes in the quenched intensity of GQDs@MnO2-NS conjugates in presence of several interfering agents with lactoferrin. In addition, other analytical features including reproducibility and stability of fabricated GQDs@MnO2-NS biosensors were also evaluated. For repeatability, six GQDs@MnO2-NS biosensors were prepared using a reported method and then used for detection of lactoferrin (200 ng/mL) using a developed sensing procedure in triplicates. After that, the stability of the prepared GQDs@MnO2-NS biosensor was measured at 25 °C for 7 days using 200 ng/mL of lactoferrin concentration in triplicates.

The GQDs were formed utilizing hydrothermal technique with wood apple shells as a green precursor. The color of the powder of wood apple shells suspension changed from yellowish-brown to dark brown during the hydrothermal process, ensuring the synthesis of GQDs. Herein, the synthesized GQDs were observed in the UV cabinet (Fig. 2
A) and it seemed pale yellow in visible light (Fig. 2 A-i). Moreover, it showed green fluorescence (Fig. 2 A-ii) under λmax 254 nm light and bright fluorescence under UV light λmax 365 nm (Fig. 2 A-iii). Importantly, a color alteration was observed at different light and wavelengths which may be because of π-π* and n-π* that are associated with the absorption band of carboxylic functionality. Besides, the different sizes of GQDs and their band gaps resulted in color changes of GQDs at 254 nm and 365 nm [39]. Therefore, synthesized GQDs material had fluorescence in longer UV wavelengths which verified the successful synthesis GQDs. After proof of fluorescence of GQDs, freshly produced GQDs were assured by UV–visible spectroscopy (Fig. 2 B). The addition of a known amount of prepared MnO2-NS into a solution of GQDs shows the strong quenching of GQDs fluorescence intensity obtained at 365 nm wavelength (UV compartment). The UV vis band of GQDs showed a weak peak for the n-π* transition of the C=O bond (sp2 domains) at 330 nm [40]. The typical absorption point at 280 nm confirmed the π-π* transition because of aromatic sp2 domains. [41] As per the literature, prepared MnO2-NS showed maximum absorption in the UV region[31] that confirming the optical absorption characteristics of MnO2-NS. The absorption spectra of MnO2-NS in this investigation exhibited a significant maximum absorbance from 300 nm to 600 nm, with a distinctive absorbance centered at 380 nm that was attributable to the d-d shift of Mn4+ ions. Furthermore, the absorption spectrum of the GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe demonstrated the reduction in GQDs absorption peak at 280 nm (Fig. 2 B). Whereas the prepared nanoprobe showed a wide absorption peak at the center of 350 nm that it shows a reduction in absorption as compared to the bare nanosheets. As per literature, it highly overlaps the fluorescent emission spectrum of synthesized GQDs [31]. It confirmed the formation of fluorescent GQDs@MnO2-NS-based nanoprobe. The fluorescence excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of green-produced GQDs were found to be about 325 and 410 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 C). The fluorescence spectrum of eco-friendly produced GQDs was examined using a spectrofluorometer at regulated room temperature at multiple excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2 C). In brief, the figure depicted a broad photoluminescence bandwidth with the appropriate excitation wavelengths (310 nm to 325 nm). As a result, GQDs photoluminescence emission peaks were red and shifted from 402.5 nm to 410 nm. When the excitation wavelength is 325 nm, the emission peak at 410 nm exhibits the maximum fluorescence intensity. This maximum fluorescence intensity is perhaps owing to the π-π* transition. While the excitation wavelength was increased from 325 nm to 360 nm, the fluorescence emission intensity decreases with an increase in emission wavelength as per the literature reported [42]. Therefore, the optimized excitation wavelength is 325 nm, which showed maximum fluorescence emission intensity at 410 nm emission wavelength[43]. Possibly, it may be because of the surface states of GQDs [44], electronic conjugated structures, available zigzag sites [45], and emissive traps [44,45]. The percent quantum yield of newly produced GQDs was determined to be 47.50 percent, with quinine sulfate serving as a reference.

The particle size investigation, PDI, and zeta potential measurement of prepared GQDs, MnO2-NS, and GQDs@MnO2-NS were confirmed via a particle size analyzer. In brief, the particle size and particle density index (PDI) of freshly produced GQDs were revealed to be 43.5 nm and 0.216 nm, accordingly (Fig. 3
A). It showcased that nanosized GQDs dispersed consistently in prepared samples. The particle size and PDI of obtained MnO2-NS were reported to be 130.9 nm and 0.251, respectively, owing to uniform distributions of nanosheets in the obtained MnO2-NS dispersion (Fig. 3 B). The particle size of the conjugated GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe was reported to be 200.2 nm. When compared to individual GQDs and MnO2-NS, the particle size was depicted to increase in this study. It might be due to the emergence of a conjugate between GQDs and MnO2-NS (Fig. 3 C).

Importantly, GQDs have a zeta potential of –34.55 mV, indicating the presence of oxygen-containing functions such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and so on. Furthermore, it validated the synthesis of GQDs with high water stability (Fig. 4
A). The MnO2-NS demonstrated the negative zeta potential (−27.29 mV) (Fig. 4 B), validating the nanosheet's water stability. After formation conjugate, the zeta potential of GQDs@MnO2-NS was reported to be lower (–14.70 mV) as compared to bare GQDs and MnO2-NS (Fig. 4 C). It denotes the production of a GQDs@MnO2-NS centered conjugate. Electrostatic connection is unlikely due to the negative potential of both GQDs and MnO2-NS. As a result of the coordination of GQDs with deprotonated functional groups with manganese ions (Mn2+), conjugate formation is feasible. Furthermore, van der Waals forces involving MnO2-NS and GQDs should serve an essential function in the sorption of GQDs in MnO2-NS [46]. FTIR analysis of the conjugate was undertaken for additional assurance.

Fig. 5
A displays the FTIR of GQDs, MnO2-Ns, and final GQDs@MnO2- Ns composites. In brief, FTIR spectra of GQDs demonstrated the peak at 3317 cm−1, 2993.52 cm−1, 1631.77 cm−1, and 1130.28 cm−1, assured the occurrence of –OH, =C-H, C = O, and C-O stretching vibrations, respectively. Accordingly, it confirmed the existence of oxygen-based functionality on the surface of synthesized GQDs. Fig. 5 B depicts the FT-IR spectrum of prepared MnO2-NS. Herein, peaks at 3317 cm−1 and 1643.35 cm−1 confirmed the –OH and C=O stretching vibration mode which may be because of absorbed water molecules. Besides this, the absorption peak at 536.21 cm−1 assured the vibrations of Mn-O. Overall, it confirmed the synthesis of MnO2-based nanosheets. The FTIR spectra of conjugated GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe demonstrate the absorption peak at 3317.15 cm−1, 2985.80 cm−1, 1678.06 cm−1, 1338.59 cm−1, 1072.42 cm−1, and 543.92 cm−1. It may be because of the presence of OH stretching vibrations, C-H stretching vibrations, C=O stretching vibrations, CH3 bending vibrations, C-O stretching vibrations, and Mn-O bending mode vibrations, respectively (Fig. 5 C). The peak intensity of carboxy groups was displayed to be lower in this study when compared to plain GQDs and MnO2-NS, possibly due to a weak interaction amongst them. Therefore, FTIR spectra validate the fabrication of GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe.

The surface morphology and particle size of synthesized GQDs, MnO2-NS, and GQDs@MnO2-NS are portrayed in Fig. 6 . In concise, Fig. 4 A demonstrated the spherical shape of uniformly distributed GQDs with an average diameter of 6.8 nm. Furthermore, HRTEM pictures verified that GQDs were distributed uniformly in water. Fig. 6 B depicts the ultrathin lamellar nanosheets of prepared MnO2-NS. Herein, MnO2-NS demonstrates the occasional folds and wrinkles with uniform distribution. In addition, an average lateral dimension of prepared MnO2-NS was found to be 89 nm. Fig. 6 C shows the HRTEM images of GQDs@MnO2-NS that confirmed the decoration of synthesized GQDs on the surface of MnO2-NS with uniform distribution of GQDs. In ending, HRTEM images confirmed the flourishing formation of the GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of lactoferrin using GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of GQDs in this investigation illustrate excitation maxima over 325 nm and emission maxima near 410 nm. After that, the concentration of MnO2-NS was optimized for fabrication of GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe using different concentrations. Herein, 100 µg/mL was found to be an optimized concentration of MnO2-NS that provides the complete quenching of GQDs fluorescence intensity (Fig. 7
A). The addition of MnO2-NS with different concentrations demonstrated the quenching of GQDs fluorescence (Turn-Off). Fig. 7 B depicts the relation of quenching efficiency (F0-F/F0, wherein F0 and F are the fluorescence of synthesized GQDs after and before the addition of MnO2-NS and the concentration of MnO2-NS (µg/mL). It establishes a linear link between the concentration of MnO2-NS and the quenching of the fluorescence of green-produced GQDs. Fluorescence quenching was perhaps attributable to IFE. Furthermore, the absorbance of MnO2 nanosheets may be used to modulate the fluorescence intensity of GQDs using the IFE and fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanisms. It might be due to the small distance between donor and acceptor, as well as spectrum overlap [47]. Herein, GQDs embedded with MnO2-NS can reduce the distance among them [48]. Furthermore, the UV–vis absorption spectra of ecofriendly produced GQDs and the excitation and emission spectrum of GQDs overlapping. The sensitive and easy optical monitoring platform depended on MnO2-NS and greenly generated GQDs as the energy donor-acceptor pair, having GQDs acting as donors and MnO2-NS acting as an energy acceptor. The zeta potential study confirmed alterations in zeta potential in the presence of nanosheets in produced GQDs, which might be due to weak van der Waal association and, as a result, quenching of GQDs fluorescence intensity [35].

The fabricated GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe was further used for sensing lactoferrin. In this study, the fluorescence “Turn-On” (recovery of signals) was evaluated as a function of the lactoferrin concentration. It provides information about GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe sensitivity to sense lactoferrin in provided samples. Herein, a range of variety 5 ng/mL to 1600 ng/mL lactoferrin solutions were tested under ideal conditions by addition to quenched GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe. Curiously, elevating the quantity of lactoferrin progressively re-established the fluorescence strength of the GQDs@MnO2-NS nanosensor (Fig. 8
A). The wide concentration range was found to be wide from 5 to 1600 ng/mL whereas the calibration curve based on fluorescence intensity vs concentration of lactoferrin (Fig. 8 B) from 5 to 100 ng/mL (y = 4.036 + 261.54, R2 = 0.991) whereas the LOD and LOQ was found to be 1.69 ng/mL and 5.12 ng/mL, in that order. Finally, as contrasted to prior described approaches, the suggested biosensor has good sensitivity. The real-time analysis in spiked artificial saliva employing the anticipated GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe shows 97.7 % and 95.41 % recovery for both 10 ng/mL and 100 ng/mL concentrations of lactoferrin, respectively. In this, % RSD was found to be 0.35 % and 0.72 % assuring the practical applicability of fabricated GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe for lactoferrin measurement in saliva (Table 1 ).

Scheme 1 depicts the schematic presentation of the sensing mechanism involved in lactoferrin sensing using the designed GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe. In brief, sensing of lactoferrin involved the fluorescent turn “On-Off-On” detection mechanism wherein initial high-intensity fluorescence intensity of synthesized GQDs termed as fluorescence “Turn-On”. The addition of MnO2-NS into fluorescent GQDs resulted in quenching of fluorescence intensity that is termed fluorescence “Turn-Off”. Finally, the addition of lactoferrin into GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe quenched fluorescent resulted in regaining, which is called fluorescence “Turn-On”. A literature survey revealed that the IFE, static quenching and dynamic quenching effect are the main ones responsible for quenching fluorescence. Furthermore, because of the negative zeta potential of both GQDs and MnO2-NS, the distance between them was rather long. It is primarily caused by electrical repulsion. As a result, there was no fluorescence resonance energy transfer. In a nutshell, when using the GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe to measure lactoferrin, the IFE will be the dominant sensing mechanism [32]. On the other hand, responsive and efficient optical detecting systems employed via MnO2-NS and newly produced GQDs as the energy donor-acceptor pairing. After the addition of lactoferrin, the fluorescence is getting recovered which may be because of MnO2- NS decomposition to Mn2+ or Mn3+. Herein, Mn3+ has been reported as water unstable as well as a strong oxidant as compared to Mn2+. Therefore, we conclude that the key reductive product is Mn2+ after the decomposition of MnO2-NS [32]. Laboratory identification assays were utilized to confirm the occurrence of Mn2+ in the supernatant [49,50]. After the reaction was completed, the lactoferrin and GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe combination was centrifuged. The addition of aqueous sodium hydroxide (NaOH) to the supernatant of the mixture produced the buff white precipitate (equation 1), ensuring the formation of manganese hydroxide [Mn(OH)2]. Following that, exposure of Mn(OH)2 to bromine water (oxidizing agent) resulted in the black precipitate of MnO2 (equation 2). Essentially, it ensured the existence of Mn2+ ions in the liquid once the redox process was completed.
(1)
Mn
2 +
+ 2 N a O H → M n
( O H )
2
+ 2
Na
+
(2)
2 M n
( O H )
2
+
O 2
→ 2 M n
O 2
+ 2
H 2
O

The addition of 1600 ng/mL MnO2-NS resulted in the highest fluorescence intensity of GQDs, but the addition of the next high concentration culminated in no further increment in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. In short, MnO2-NS offers a huge surface area, strong/broad optical absorption, strong oxidation capability, and catalytic action that added more advantages for sensing lactoferrin with high sensitivity.

To boost the sensing potential of the prepared GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe, the anti-interference potential was investigated. Briefly, the selectivity for lactoferrin in presence of different interfering agents mainly metal ions and biomolecules (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Zn2+, urea, uric acid, glucose, ascorbic acid, cysteine, lactoferrin, etc.) were confirmed based on the recovery of GQDs fluorescence. Fig. 9 depicts the selectivity performance of fabricated GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe. As a result, the GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe demonstrates that the prepared concentration of lactoferrin generated fluorescence recovery of GQDs from quenched GQDs@MnO2-NS. Moreover, the presence of cystine demonstrated a slight fluorescence recovery. Overall, the prepared nanosystem depicts the high selectivity towards the lactoferrin in the GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe. In the case of repeatability of biosensors, the percent relative standard deviation (% RSD) was obtained to be 1.54 % confirming the repeatability of the biosensor. In the case of stability study, the % RSD was found to be 1.28 % upto 6 days at preferred experimental conditions whereas the % RSD was increased (more than 5 %) after 6 days. Therefore, the prepared GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe shows good stability upto 6 days. In conclusion, the proposed GQDs@MnO2-NS-based fluorescent biosensor exhibited good stability and reproducibility.

In this work, we designed the simplistic, highly sensitive, selective, and low expensive GQDs@MnO2-NS based fluorescent turn “Off-On” nanoprobe for sensing periodontal disease biomarkers namely lactoferrin. In brief, fluorescent GQDs were synthesized using powder of Wood apple shell waste as a green precursor by a one-step, eco-friendly and simplistic hydrothermal method. Herein, obtained GQDs demonstrate the superior fluorescent intensity and sensing ability in presence of lactoferrin. After that, MnO2-NS were prepared through a template-free approach and further used as a quenching agent for fabrication of the GQDs@MnO2-NS mediated fluorescent turn “Off-On” sensor. The incorporation of MnO2-NS furnishes the quenching of fluorescence intensity of GQDs through inner filter effects whereas GQDs and MnO2-NS strong interaction between also essential for quenching of fluorescence. Interestingly, the addition of different concentrations of lactoferrin into GQDs@MnO2-NS destroyed MnO2-NS. Moreover, it resulted in reappears of the fluorescence intensity of GQDs, which may be because of the redox reaction between lactoferrin and prepared MnO2-NS. It provides a wide concentration range from 5 ng/mL to 1600 ng/mL and a low LOD of 1.69 ng/mL. The anti-interference analysis demonstrated that the GQDs@MnO2-NS nanoprobe sensor is pretty selective for lactoferrin in the existence of multiple interfering chemicals. Furthermore, it demonstrated high stability and reproducibility. As a result, our constructed GQDs@MnO2-NS centered fluorescence switch on nanoprobe design will enable a novel substitute for conventionally utilized methodologies for lactoferrin detection, which will aid in the diagnosis of periodontal disorders.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur for providing the necessary facilities.
